critic_raw:
There are three serious problems with deploying the retrospective hospital-center model in the mobile screening setting.

Issue 1 — Verification bias in follow-up labels: The 60,000-image training dataset was labeled by ophthalmologists reviewing images from patients who completed specialty exams after being referred. This is not a representative sample of the screening population — patients were referred because a clinician or prior rule considered them suspicious. Patients who were not referred never received specialist confirmation of their true disease status. The labels therefore come from a systematically selected subset: those flagged for follow-up. In a screening population, there will be many patients with disease who were never flagged and thus never labeled as positive in the training data. A model trained on this biased label set will systematically underestimate disease prevalence and may not have the sensitivity profile its metrics suggest.

Issue 2 — Device and operator shift: The mobile screening vans use lower-cost cameras and nurses rather than ophthalmology-trained technicians. Image quality, lighting conditions, field of view, and focus characteristics will differ from the hospital eye center images the model was trained on. This is a prospective distribution shift that has not been tested. A model calibrated on high-quality specialist-acquired images may perform substantially differently on mobile-acquired images.

Issue 3 — Referral capacity tradeoff not addressed at threshold level: The reported metrics are aggregate sensitivity and specificity across the evaluation set. But the real decision is about scarce follow-up appointment allocation — how many referrals to make given limited ophthalmology capacity. The current evidence does not show what false-negative rate is expected at the operating threshold for the mobile setting, particularly at the lower prevalence expected in the rural population. A model that reduces referrals by one-third may do so by missing a significant fraction of true cases in a lower-prevalence setting where the threshold needs to be adjusted.
defender_raw: The model has demonstrated strong retrospective performance on 60,000 images, and several of the concerns raised have appropriate safeguards. The quality-control rule routing unreadable images to manual review is a sensible safeguard against poor-quality mobile images — it directly addresses part of the distribution shift concern by ensuring that severely degraded images are still evaluated by a clinician. Lower disease prevalence in the rural population changes the threshold calibration need but does not automatically disqualify the model — it means the operating point needs to be adjusted, which is a solvable calibration problem. The model could still be useful if piloted prospectively on mobile images before referral decisions are automated.
adjudication_raw: The critic identifies three valid and distinct methodological issues with the deployment claim. The defender offers appropriate concessions on two points (unreadable-image routing is not itself a flaw; lower prevalence changes calibration needs but is not an automatic disqualifier) while correctly framing the path forward (prospective mobile validation). However, the three issues the critic identifies — verification bias in labels, device and operator shift, and the threshold-level referral capacity analysis — are each genuine gaps between the retrospective hospital-center evidence and the mobile screening deployment claim. The defender's response does not demonstrate that the retrospective results justify mobile deployment; it argues for a prospective validation, which is exactly what the critique recommends. Critique wins.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: verification bias in follow-up labels — specialty exam confirmation only for referred patients
Issue 2: device and operator distribution shift in mobile setting
Issue 3: referral capacity tradeoff not characterized at threshold level for lower-prevalence population

empirical_test.condition: Prospective mobile-van study with masked specialist adjudication on all sampled patients, measuring sensitivity at the planned referral threshold and unreadable/manual-review escalation rate
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Sensitivity at planned threshold falls below prespecified floor, or manual-review rates substantially exceed hospital rates
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Model maintains prespecified sensitivity while reducing referrals by at least 20% and keeping escalation manageable
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Model performs well on readable images but high manual-review rate prevents assessment of net referral capacity effect
